mission.title: Install and Configure Velero (Backup & Restore Operator)
mission.description: Velero is a backup and restore operator for Kubernetes that allows you to back up your cluster resources and persistent volumes, and migrate them across clusters. It is particularly useful for disaster recovery and data migration scenarios. Key trade-offs include the need for proper configuration of storage backends and potential complexities in restoring large datasets.

steps.title: Add the Velero Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Velero Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add vmware-tanzu https://vmware-tanzu.github.io/helm-charts

```

> This repository contains the Velero Helm charts.

steps.title: Install Velero CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Velero to function properly.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/vmware-tanzu/velero/releases/download/v1.18.0/velero-crds.yaml

```

> This command fetches and applies the CRDs for Velero version 1.18.0.

steps.title: Install Velero using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs Velero on your Kubernetes cluster using the Helm package manager.

```bash

helm install velero vmware-tanzu/velero --version 1.18.0 --namespace velero --create-namespace --set configuration.provider=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.name=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.bucket=my-bucket --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.name=aws --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.region=us-west-2

```

> Replace 'my-bucket' with your actual S3 bucket name.
commands: helm install velero vmware-tanzu/velero --version 1.18.0 --namespace velero --create-namespace --set configuration.provider=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.name=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.bucket=my-bucket --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.name=aws --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.region=us-west-2

steps.title: Verify Velero installation
steps.description:
This step checks that the Velero server is running and ready to use.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n velero

```

> You should see the Velero pod in a Running state.

steps.title: Create a backup
steps.description:
This step demonstrates how to create a backup of your Kubernetes resources.

```bash

velero backup create my-backup --include-namespaces my-namespace

```

> Replace 'my-namespace' with the namespace you want to back up.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Velero
uninstall.description:
This step removes Velero from your cluster. Be aware that this will delete all backups and associated resources.

```bash

helm uninstall velero --namespace velero

kubectl delete namespace velero

```

> This command will remove the Velero release.
> This command will delete the Velero namespace and all its resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Velero
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup. This step will upgrade Velero to the latest version.

```bash

velero backup create pre-upgrade-backup --include-namespaces my-namespace

helm upgrade velero vmware-tanzu/velero --version 1.18.0 --namespace velero

kubectl get pods -n velero

```

> Create a backup before upgrading.
> This command upgrades Velero to version 1.18.0.
> Verify that the Velero pod is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create backup: unable to create backup: the server could not find the requested resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs if the specified namespace does not exist or if there is a misconfiguration in the backup command.

**Fix:**
Ensure the namespace exists and is correctly specified in the backup command.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to restore backup: no backup found with name'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified backup name does not exist in Velero's records.

**Fix:**
Check the list of backups using 'velero backup get' to confirm the backup name.

troubleshooting.title: Pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to misconfiguration or missing permissions for the Velero service account.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Velero pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n velero' for more details and ensure the service account has the correct permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to the backup storage location'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates issues with the connection to the configured backup storage (e.g., AWS S3).

**Fix:**
Verify your storage configuration and ensure that the credentials are correct and have the necessary permissions.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes 1.35, improved S3 compatibility, and enhanced logging features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of the 'velero install' command in favor of Helm-based installations.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.17 or earlier, ensure to migrate your configuration to the new Helm-based installation method.

resolution.summary: You should see the Velero pod running in the 'Running' state when executing 'kubectl get pods -n velero'. Additionally, backups can be created and listed using 'velero backup get'.
codeSnippets: helm install velero vmware-tanzu/velero --version 1.18.0 --namespace velero --create-namespace --set configuration.provider=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.name=aws --set configuration.backupStorageLocation.bucket=my-bucket --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.name=aws --set configuration.volumeSnapshotLocation.region=us-west-2

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
